questions.explanation: Android Studio is the official IDE for Android development, providing editing, building, running, and debugging tools.

options.A: Designing and building Android apps using an IDE with tools like code editor, emulator, and profilers
options.B: Hosting Android apps in the cloud for deployment
options.C: Replacing the Android operating system on a device

questions.question: What is Android Studio primarily used for in mobile app development?

questions.explanation: The Android SDK includes libraries (APIs) and tools (like adb, build tools) required to build, run, and test Android apps.

options.A: A set of APIs, platform tools, and build tools needed to develop and test Android apps
options.B: A database server for storing user data remotely
options.C: A UI framework that only supports iOS
options.D: A version control system for managing source code

questions.question: What does the Android SDK provide to developers?

questions.explanation: Kotlin uses 'val' for read-only references and 'var' for mutable references.
questions.question: In Kotlin, which keyword declares a read-only (immutable) variable reference?

questions.explanation: Git is a widely used version control system, a core tool for software configuration management.
questions.question: Which tool is most directly associated with software configuration management for tracking code changes and collaboration?

questions.explanation: applicationId and minSdk are configured in the module (app) Gradle file inside android { defaultConfig { ... } }.

options.B: The module-level build.gradle(.kts) file under the android { defaultConfig { ... } } block

questions.question: In Gradle for Android, where do you typically set the app's applicationId and minSdk version?

questions.explanation: @Composable marks functions that participate in Compose's UI rendering and recomposition system.

options.A: It marks a function as a UI-building function that can emit Compose UI elements
options.B: It forces a function to run on a background thread
options.C: It converts JSON into Kotlin data classes automatically
options.D: It enables SQL queries to be validated at compile time

questions.question: In Jetpack Compose, what is the primary purpose of the @Composable annotation?

questions.explanation: Coroutines with suspend functions (commonly with Retrofit) allow non-blocking network calls, typically dispatched on Dispatchers.IO.

options.A: Run the network call on the main thread and catch exceptions
options.B: Use Kotlin coroutines and call the API from a suspend function (e.g., via Retrofit) on an IO dispatcher
options.C: Use Thread.sleep() to wait for the response
options.D: Store the URL in SharedPreferences and let Android fetch it automatically

questions.question: You need to fetch data from a REST API without blocking the UI thread. Which approach is most appropriate in a modern Android app using Kotlin?

questions.explanation: ViewModel is lifecycle-aware and survives configuration changes, making it the correct place to keep UI state and data streams.

options.A: Use a ViewModel to hold state and expose it via StateFlow/LiveData; collect it in the UI
options.B: Store all UI state in local variables inside the Composable
options.C: Put the state in an Activity companion object
options.D: Write the state to a file on every recomposition

questions.question: A Compose screen collects a Flow from a ViewModel. After rotating the device, the UI restarts and the data reloads unnecessarily. Which Jetpack component and pattern best helps retain UI-related state across configuration changes?

questions.explanation: Room requires version bumps and migrations for schema changes; adding a non-null column typically needs a default/backfill to keep existing rows valid.

options.A: Increase the database version and provide a proper Room migration that adds the column with a default value (or backfills data)
options.B: Delete the app's database file on startup
options.D: Keep the version the same so Room won't notice the change

questions.question: You are using Room for local persistence. You add a new non-null column to an existing table in production. What is the most robust way to prevent crashes on upgrade while preserving user data?

questions.explanation: SingletonComponent with @Singleton ensures one instance for the app process, which is the standard approach for app-wide repositories.

options.A: Annotate the repository with @ActivityScoped and inject it into Activities only
options.B: Provide/bind the repository in a Hilt module installed in SingletonComponent and annotate it with @Singleton
options.C: Create the repository with 'new' inside every Composable to ensure freshness
options.D: Use a static global variable without Hilt to avoid dependency graphs

questions.question: In an Android app using Hilt, you want a single instance of a repository shared across the entire app process. Which setup is correct?
